title: Chapter 44: The Meeting
content: Dr. Lena Vasquez arrives at Patel's clinic at 6:15 PM, fourteen minutes early, which means she's the kind of person who thinks early is on time and on time is late, which means Chloe is already tired.

It's not that Chloe doesn't like organized people. Organized people are fine. Organized people have calendars and planners and systems. But there's something about someone who shows up early to a meeting about *studying you* that feels a little too much like a predator who's really committed to the pre-game.

Which is unfair. Chloe knows it's unfair. She's been thinking about this meeting for three days, ever since Patel mentioned that his colleague from Meridian Biotech wanted to discuss a long-term research plan, and in those three days she's cycled through about eleven emotional states ranging from *absolutely not* to *well, maybe* to *who am I even kidding, I'm going to at least hear her out because that's what a reasonable capybara would do.*

Dave is here. Patel is here. The clinic is closed — Patel locked up at six, turned off the fluorescent hum that makes his waiting room feel like a governmentbuilding simulation, and set out chairs in a loose circle in the back exam room. It's the same room where Chloe had her first check-up, the same table with the crinkly paper that she absolutely destroyed with her claws. There's a laptop on a rolling cart, positioned so Chloe can reach the keyboard. There's a plate of orange slices on the counter because Patel is, at his core, a man who believes every difficult conversation is improved by citrus.

Lena Vasquez is forty-three, five-foot-six, and wearing a blazer that costs more than Maya's rent. She has dark hair pulled back in a clip, reading glasses pushed up on her head, and the kind of posture that suggests she could bench-press her own body weight if the situation required it. Her handshake with Dave is firm. Her handshake with Patel is brief and familiar — they know each other, clearly, from some professional context that involved conferences and probably wine.
Then she turns to Chloe.
And here's the thing that matters: she speaks to Chloe directly.

"Chloe." Not to Dave. Not to Patel. Not to the capybara while making eye contact with the humans, the way you'd talk to a dog while looking at the owner. She looks at Chloe. "I'm Dr. Lena Vasquez. I understand you can communicate through typing. I'll wait as long as you need."
She sits down. She doesn't lean forward. She doesn't smile the sales-pitch smile. She just sits, and waits, and lets the silence be what it is.
Chloe looks at her for a long time.

Lena doesn't fidget. She doesn't check her phone. She doesn't fill the space with explanations or justifications. She exists in the silence the way Chloe exists in a room — deliberately, completely, without apology.
Chloe respects that. She also doesn't trust it. The most dangerous people aren't the ones who can't sit still. They're the ones who can.
She walks to the laptop cart. Flexes her nose. Types.
*Hi.*
"Hi," Lena says.
*You can call me Chloe. Not "the subject." Not "the specimen." Chloe.*
"Chloe," Lena repeats. Like it's a name, not a label. Like she's meeting a person and not a data point.

The research plan is laid out in a folder that Lena places on the counter next to the orange slices. It's thirty-two pages. Chloe can't read it — not because she can't read, she can read fine, but because turning pages with a capybara nose is an exercise in frustration and also one of the orange slices is really calling to her — so Lena summarizes.

"Non-invasive imaging first. fMRI, PET, whatever your comfort level allows. We map the neurological changes — compare your current brain structure to baseline models for both human and capybara cognition. Blood draws — standard panels, plus anything unusual that comes up. Behavioral observation, two to three sessions a week, here or at your apartment. Whichever you prefer."
Chloe types: *And if I say no to something?*
"Then we don't do it. Full consent, full stop, any time, any procedure."
*What about psych evaluation?*

Lena doesn't flinch. "Standard cognitive and emotional assessment. Administered by someone you choose from a list of three practitioners I'll provide. All confidential. All designed to understand how your consciousness adapted, not whether it's valid."
The word *adapted* does something in Chloe's chest. Not a pang exactly. A recognition. Like finding a word for a feeling she'd been sitting with for months.
*How long?*

"Twelve months. Split time between the apartment and our Boston facility — you'd have a dedicated habitat, designed to your specifications. Not a lab. A living space. You'd have visitation from anyone you want, anytime."
*Money?*

Lena glances at Dave. Dave shrugs — *go ahead, she asked*. "$500,000 over the study period. Plus health insurance. Plus living expenses. Plus a relocation stipend if Boston becomes part of the rotation."
Half a million dollars.

Chloe lets that number sit in the room. It's enough to change everything. It's enough to pay off Maya's student loans, to give Dave breathing room, to fund the sit-ins, to — no. That's not how this works. The money would be for Chloe. For her participation. For her time. For her *body*, essentially, and the data it produces in a world where her body is already the most examined thing on the internet.
*What does Meridian get?*
"Publication rights. Conference presentations. The usual academic profile boost from a landmark study."
*Branding?*
"No." Lena says it flatly. No hedging. "No merchandise. No social media campaigns. No 'Chloe the Capybara presented by Meridian Biotech.' I'm a scientist. I don't do branding."

Dave shifts in his chair. He's been quiet, which is his version of screaming. Chloe can feel him vibrating with the desire to ask seventeen questions, none of which are his to ask. She appreciates the restraint. It's new.
*Why do you want to study me?*
The question sits on the screen. Lena reads it. She takes a breath — not a performance breath, not a dramatic pause, just the kind of breath you take before saying something true.

"Because what happened to you shouldn't be possible. A human consciousness in a non-human body. Full cognitive preservation. Apparent emotional continuity. That breaks every model we have. Every assumption about the relationship between mind and brain, about what's essential and what's substrate-dependent." She pauses. "I'm not interested in what you are, Chloe. I'm interested in how you got there. Because that knowledge could help people who don't have your... advantage."
Advantage.
The word lands like a coin hitting the floor. Small. Metallic. Spinning before it settles.

Chloe stares at it on the screen — it's still there, the cursor blinking after it like a heartbeat. *Advantage.* Is that what this is? Is peace an advantage? Is the ability to sit in a room and feel the warmth of a blanket and the taste of an orange and the presence of a friend and call that enough — is that an advantage? Or is it just... the thing that's left when you strip away everything that was screaming?

And then: is choosing it a luxury? Because there are people who can't choose. People whose brains won't let them sit still, whose nervous systems treat every moment like a threat, whose *advantage* is just surviving the day. The Method works for Chloe. But Chloe started from a place of choosing to give up. That's not an advantage. That's a beginning that happened to have nowhere to go but somewhere else.
*I need to think,* Chloe types.

"Of course." Lena stands. She reaches into her blazer pocket and produces a business card — simple, white, just a name and number. She places it on the counter next to the folder. Next to the oranges. "Everything is in the proposal. Take your time. Call me when you're ready. Or don't. Either way, it was good to meet you, Chloe."
She leaves. The door closes. The clinic settles.
Dave exhales like he's been holding his breath for forty-five minutes.
"She's good," he says. Not a compliment. An observation. The way you'd say *that knife is sharp.*

Chloe doesn't type a response. She eats an orange slice. Then another. The juice runs down her chin and drips onto the exam table crinkly paper and it's good, it's so good, the specific sweetness that only oranges have, the one that makes everything else quieter for just a moment.
"Chlo."
She looks at him.

"I need to tell you something." Dave's voice is careful. His *I have information and I don't know how you'll react* voice. He uses it maybe four times a year. "Greenline Press DM'd me. About a book deal."
Chloe's nose hovers over the keyboard.
*What kind of book?*

"Not a branding thing. A book. About the community, the method, the philosophy. Your story — our story. They want a proposal first, but they're already talking advance. Around sixty thousand. Over two years."
Sixty thousand dollars.

Not half a million. Not life-changing, debt-erasing, problem-solving money. But sixty thousand. Enough to shrink the mountain. Enough to breathe. Enough to recognize the shape of a life that doesn't involve choosing between groceries and the electric bill.
*Who would write it?*
"You. Through me, or through Maya, or however you want to do it. They know the situation. They're flexible."
Flexible. A word that means *we know you're a capybara and we're willing to work with that.* A word that means *your story matters more than the format it arrives in.*

Chloe thinks about two paths. One fast and scientific, paved with imaging machines and blood draws and a half-million dollars and the weight of being studied, documented, known in a way that can never be unknown. One slow and personal, built on words and memory and the quiet accumulation of pages that say *this is what happened and this is what I learned and this is what I'm still figuring out.*
Both of them cost something. Both of them give something. Neither is safe.
*I need to think about that too,* she types.

"I know." Dave picks up an orange slice. Eats it. They sit in Patel's quiet clinic and chew in tandem, capybara and human, and the silence is what the silence always is when they're together. Not empty. Full of the specific warmth of two people who have stopped trying to fix each other and started just being nearby.
After a while, Chloe types one more thing.
*She wasn't wrong, you know. About the advantage.*
Dave looks at the screen. Looks at her.
"I don't know if I'd call it an advantage," he says. "I think I'd call it a superpower that looks like doing nothing."
Chloe considers this. Then she eats another orange slice, because some questions don't have answers and some answers are just fruit, and right now the fruit is enough.
